original_abstract:
After a protracted era of impunity, the last twenty years have seen a rapid "institutionalization" and "judicialization" of international criminal justice. This transformation has been driven primarily by the emergence of formal international and hybrid tribunals tasked with ending the historical lack of accountability for mass atrocities. However, this shift forced judges to adjudicate deeply complex and emotionally charged cases with very little established practical experience to draw upon.

More recently, the validity of several landmark decisions has come under intense public scrutiny, triggered in part by rare and pointed criticisms from within the judiciary itself. Highly visible controversies have exposed the inherent strains in the judicial decision-making process, raising fundamental questions about the "efficiency" and integrity of the justice being delivered. This article examines the legal frameworks used to interpret and apply rules in the international criminal arena. It further explores how these legal processes are being increasingly tested by a volatile mix of internal pressures and external factors, ranging from the specific context of the conflicts themselves to their broader political and social fallout.
humanized_abstract:
In many respects, the last twenty years can be seen as the end of an era of absolute impunity in relation to international crimes. After two decades of rapid institutionalization and judicialization of international criminal justice, national courts are no longer the only players in the field. The growth of international and hybrid courts has not only filled a gap in relation to historical crimes but has also put national judiciary in a position to be tried and tested. In any event, the first two decades of the ad hoc tribunals indicated that international criminal justice was more than a paper exercise and that some of the most horrific crimes could one day be the object of detailed legal investigation. The task of adjudicating upon these crimes fell then on to the shoulders of judges. In doing so, they had to work with evidence that was brand new and cases that were utterly unprecedented, in a context where they had not had any opportunity to put into practice their skills and professional judgement in relation to the most odious of crimes.

Lately, a number of constitutional court decisions have come under scrutiny. A rare combination of criticism from inside the judiciary and public controversy has highlighted the tension between the conflicting objectives of the judicial decision-making process, turning into a major debate about the efficiency and effectiveness of the administration of justice. This article deals with legal issues of proof and procedure in international criminal law and its developing fields of application. The increasing challenges within the judicial process itself as well as external forces such as characteristics of the underlying conflicts and their repercussions upon the social and political bodies of states are examined.
